title: AP U.S. History MCQ Practice Exam 07

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Secondary-source interpretation. A historian argues that some Native societies in the Southwest before sustained European contact developed irrigation systems, settled villages, and exchange networks that allowed farming communities to survive in arid environments. Which evidence would best support this interpretation?

choices.A: British shipping records showing tobacco exports from Virginia
choices.B: Archaeological evidence of canal systems, maize remains, pottery, and village sites in desert river valleys
choices.C: Federal land surveys dividing the Northwest Territory into townships
choices.D: Newspaper reports about railroad strikes in Chicago

questions.correctAnswerText: Archaeological evidence of canal systems, maize remains, pottery, and village sites in desert river valleys
questions.explanation: Canal systems, maize, pottery, and village sites directly support claims about irrigation, farming, settlement, and exchange in the Southwest.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Secondary-source interpretation. A historian argues that Native farming communities in the Southwest used irrigation, settled villages, and exchange networks to adapt to arid environments before sustained European contact. Which factor most directly encouraged the development described?

choices.A: Heavy rainfall that eliminated the need for water management
choices.B: European demand for cotton textiles
choices.C: Access to Atlantic shipping routes
choices.D: The need to manage scarce water for agriculture

questions.correctAnswerText: The need to manage scarce water for agriculture
questions.explanation: Arid conditions encouraged irrigation and water management among Southwestern farming communities.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Secondary-source interpretation. A historian argues that Native farming communities in the Southwest used irrigation, settled villages, and exchange networks to adapt to arid environments. Which argument about precontact Native societies is best supported?

choices.A: Environmental adaptation helped produce diverse forms of settlement and economic organization.
choices.B: Native communities were unable to farm before European colonization.
choices.C: All Native societies depended on the same subsistence strategy.
choices.D: Southwestern villages were isolated from all trade networks.

questions.correctAnswerText: Environmental adaptation helped produce diverse forms of settlement and economic organization.
questions.explanation: The interpretation shows that Native societies adapted to regional environments in varied and complex ways.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated French colonial report from Louisiana, early 1700s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. “Our settlements remain thin along the river, and the strength of the colony depends on trade, military posts, and alliances with Native nations more than on fields of settlers.” Which conclusion is best supported by the report?

choices.A: French Louisiana developed a dense settler population like New England.
choices.B: French colonists rejected trade and diplomacy with Native peoples.
choices.C: French power in the lower Mississippi Valley depended heavily on trade networks, military posts, and Native alliances.
choices.D: French officials abolished all imperial competition in North America.

questions.correctAnswerText: French power in the lower Mississippi Valley depended heavily on trade networks, military posts, and Native alliances.
questions.explanation: The report emphasizes thin settlement and reliance on trade, posts, and alliances.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated French colonial report from Louisiana, early 1700s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The report says French settlements remain thin along the river and depend on trade, posts, and alliances. Which geographic feature most directly shaped the colony’s strategic importance?

choices.A: Control of the Mississippi River system
choices.B: Access to New England town meetings
choices.C: Location beside the Appalachian Proclamation Line
choices.D: Proximity to California gold fields

questions.correctAnswerText: Control of the Mississippi River system
questions.explanation: The Mississippi River gave Louisiana strategic value for trade, movement, and imperial claims.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated French colonial report from Louisiana, early 1700s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The report stresses that the colony depends on trade, military posts, and Native alliances more than dense settlement. The report’s likely purpose was to persuade French officials to do which of the following?

choices.A: Abandon the lower Mississippi Valley to British settlers
choices.B: Support imperial claims by funding posts, trade, and diplomacy
choices.C: Replace all Native alliances with elected colonial assemblies
choices.D: End French participation in North American imperial competition

questions.correctAnswerText: Support imperial claims by funding posts, trade, and diplomacy
questions.explanation: The report’s emphasis on posts and alliances suggests a request for imperial support.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated French colonial report from Louisiana, early 1700s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The report describes thin French settlement, river-based posts, trade, and Native alliances. Which comparison with British mainland colonies is best supported?

choices.A: French Louisiana generally relied less on dense settler migration than many British colonies along the Atlantic coast.
choices.B: French Louisiana had a larger settler population than all British colonies combined.
choices.C: British colonies avoided agriculture and permanent settlement.
choices.D: French and British colonies used identical settlement patterns in every region.

questions.correctAnswerText: French Louisiana generally relied less on dense settler migration than many British colonies along the Atlantic coast.
questions.explanation: The report contrasts thin French settlement with a model based on trade, posts, and Native diplomacy.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described political print, c. 1765. The image shows a coffin labeled “Liberty” being carried by colonial printers, merchants, and artisans. A stamped paper hangs above the scene, and a crowd gathers near a customs house. Which issue is most directly reflected in the print?

choices.A: Opposition to federal income taxes during the Progressive Era
choices.B: Debate over the expansion of slavery into Kansas
choices.C: Colonial resistance to the Stamp Act and parliamentary taxation
choices.D: Support for the Alien and Sedition Acts

questions.correctAnswerText: Colonial resistance to the Stamp Act and parliamentary taxation
questions.explanation: The stamped paper, printers, merchants, and “Liberty” imagery point to Stamp Act resistance.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described political print, c. 1765. A coffin labeled “Liberty” is carried by colonial printers, merchants, and artisans. A stamped paper hangs above the scene, and a crowd gathers near a customs house. Why were printers and merchants especially likely to oppose the policy represented?

choices.A: The tax directly affected paper, legal documents, newspapers, and commercial transactions.
choices.B: The tax required enslaved laborers to join the British army.
choices.C: The tax abolished all colonial trade with the Caribbean.
choices.D: The tax gave colonial assemblies complete control over imperial revenue.

questions.correctAnswerText: The tax directly affected paper, legal documents, newspapers, and commercial transactions.
questions.explanation: The Stamp Act affected printed and legal materials, making printers and merchants especially concerned.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described political print, c. 1765. The print shows “Liberty” as dead because of stamped paper, with colonial printers, merchants, artisans, and a crowd near a customs house. The likely purpose of the print was to do which of the following?

choices.A: Defend Parliament’s right to tax the colonies without protest
choices.B: Mobilize public opposition by portraying taxation as a threat to colonial liberty
choices.C: Encourage colonists to abandon printed political arguments
choices.D: Persuade colonists to accept direct British military rule

questions.correctAnswerText: Mobilize public opposition by portraying taxation as a threat to colonial liberty
questions.explanation: The print uses emotional symbolism to persuade viewers that the tax threatened liberty.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described political print, c. 1765. The image shows a coffin labeled “Liberty,” stamped paper, colonial printers and merchants, and a protesting crowd near a customs house. Which broader development is most closely connected to the image?

choices.A: The disappearance of political print culture in the colonies
choices.B: Colonial agreement that Parliament should control local taxation
choices.C: The decline of public protest after the French and Indian War
choices.D: The growth of popular political mobilization during the imperial crisis

questions.correctAnswerText: The growth of popular political mobilization during the imperial crisis
questions.explanation: The print reflects expanding public protest and political communication during the imperial crisis.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated ratification convention speech, 1788, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. “A stronger union may be necessary, but paper barriers must guard conscience, press, jury, and the people’s arms. Without declared rights, power will invent its own limits.” Which demand is most directly reflected in the speech?

choices.A: Addition of a bill of rights to the Constitution
choices.B: Immediate abolition of all state governments
choices.C: Creation of a national church
choices.D: Expansion of slavery into the Northwest Territory

questions.correctAnswerText: Addition of a bill of rights to the Constitution
questions.explanation: The speech demands declared protections for conscience, press, jury, and arms.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated ratification convention speech, 1788, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The speaker accepts that a stronger union may be necessary but warns that rights must be declared to limit power. Which group’s concerns are most directly reflected?

choices.A: Loyalists defending continued British rule
choices.B: Federalists demanding a stronger executive without limits
choices.C: Anti-Federalists worried that the new Constitution gave too much power to the national government
choices.D: Populists criticizing railroad rates

questions.correctAnswerText: Anti-Federalists worried that the new Constitution gave too much power to the national government
questions.explanation: Anti-Federalists often demanded explicit rights protections as a condition of ratification.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated ratification convention speech, 1788, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The speaker supports a stronger union only if declared rights protect conscience, press, jury, and arms. Which argument about ratification is best supported?

choices.A: Constitutional ratification involved debate over how to balance national power with protections for individual liberties.
choices.B: Anti-Federalists wanted to restore British monarchy.
choices.C: Federalists and Anti-Federalists agreed that bills of rights were unnecessary.
choices.D: Ratification debates avoided questions of civil liberties.

questions.correctAnswerText: Constitutional ratification involved debate over how to balance national power with protections for individual liberties.
questions.explanation: The speech accepts stronger union but insists on rights protections and limits.

questions.prompt:
Stimulus: Plain-text table of steamboat commerce.
Indicator | c. 1810 | c. 1840
Steamboats on western rivers | Few | Many
Downriver-only trade dependence | High | Lower
Movement of cotton, pork, flour, and passengers | Slower | Faster
River towns | Limited growth | Expanded commercial activity
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Steamboats reduced the importance of river transportation.
choices.B: Steamboats increased the speed and flexibility of commerce on western rivers.
choices.C: Steamboats ended all agricultural trade in the Mississippi Valley.
choices.D: Western river towns declined as steamboats spread.

questions.correctAnswerText: Steamboats increased the speed and flexibility of commerce on western rivers.
questions.explanation: The table shows faster movement, lower dependence on downriver trade, and expanded commerce.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of steamboat commerce: by c. 1840 steamboats on western rivers increased, trade became less dependent on downriver-only movement, goods and passengers moved faster, and river towns expanded commercial activity. Which region was most directly affected by these changes?

choices.A: The Mississippi and Ohio River valleys
choices.B: The Great Basin desert
choices.C: The Hawaiian Islands
choices.D: The New England fishing banks

questions.correctAnswerText: The Mississippi and Ohio River valleys
questions.explanation: Steamboats transformed commerce on western rivers, especially the Mississippi and Ohio systems.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of steamboat commerce: steamboats increased on western rivers by c. 1840, goods and passengers moved faster, and river towns expanded. Which broader development belongs to the same historical context?

choices.A: Decline of all western migration after 1800
choices.B: Federal prohibition of commercial agriculture in the West
choices.C: Expansion of market-oriented settlement in the trans-Appalachian West
choices.D: Creation of national-origins immigration quotas

questions.correctAnswerText: Expansion of market-oriented settlement in the trans-Appalachian West
questions.explanation: Faster river transport helped settlers and farmers connect to markets.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of steamboat commerce: steamboats expanded on western rivers, reduced downriver-only trade dependence, sped up movement of goods and passengers, and supported river-town growth. Which later development most similarly transformed national economic integration?

choices.A: The Proclamation of 1763
choices.B: The First Great Awakening
choices.C: The Dred Scott decision
choices.D: The spread of railroads across regional and national markets

questions.correctAnswerText: The spread of railroads across regional and national markets
questions.explanation: Railroads later integrated markets in a similar but broader way.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated South Carolina legislative statement, 1832, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. “A tariff that protects one section by burdening another is not the act of a limited government. A state must judge when the compact has been broken.” Which controversy is most directly reflected in the statement?

choices.A: Debate over the Equal Rights Amendment
choices.B: Conflict over Japanese American incarceration
choices.C: The Nullification Crisis
choices.D: Ratification of the Articles of Confederation

questions.correctAnswerText: The Nullification Crisis
questions.explanation: The statement’s tariff criticism and compact theory reflect South Carolina nullification arguments.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated South Carolina legislative statement, 1832, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The statement argues that a protective tariff benefits one section while burdening another. Which sectional divide is most directly reflected?

choices.A: Southern planters often opposed tariffs they believed raised costs and favored northern manufacturers.
choices.B: Northern manufacturers opposed all protective tariffs.
choices.C: Western farmers uniformly rejected internal improvements.
choices.D: New England merchants demanded the end of all foreign trade.

questions.correctAnswerText: Southern planters often opposed tariffs they believed raised costs and favored northern manufacturers.
questions.explanation: Southern critics argued protective tariffs benefited manufacturers while burdening agricultural exporters and consumers.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated South Carolina legislative statement, 1832, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The statement says a state must judge when the constitutional compact has been broken. Which argument about antebellum politics is best supported?

choices.A: Tariff debates had no connection to federalism.
choices.B: Sectional economic conflicts could become constitutional disputes over state and national authority.
choices.C: Nullification supporters wanted to abolish all state governments.
choices.D: The crisis ended all future sectional conflict.

questions.correctAnswerText: Sectional economic conflicts could become constitutional disputes over state and national authority.
questions.explanation: The tariff controversy became a debate over nullification, federal authority, and state power.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map, 1846–1848. The map shows U.S. troops moving from Texas toward northern Mexico, naval operations along the Gulf Coast and Pacific Coast, and U.S. forces entering California and New Mexico. A shaded area marks the territory acquired by the United States after the war. Which development is most directly reflected?

choices.A: The United States gained major western territories after war with Mexico.
choices.B: Mexico gained control of the Mississippi River Valley.
choices.C: The United States abandoned claims to California.
choices.D: The war ended all debate over slavery’s expansion.

questions.correctAnswerText: The United States gained major western territories after war with Mexico.
questions.explanation: The map’s campaigns and shaded acquired lands reflect the Mexican-American War and Mexican Cession.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map, 1846–1848. U.S. forces move from Texas into northern Mexico, naval operations appear on both coasts, and a shaded area marks western territory acquired after the war. Which broader ideology helped justify the expansion shown?

choices.A: Social Darwinism
choices.B: Manifest Destiny
choices.C: Containment
choices.D: The Social Gospel

questions.correctAnswerText: Manifest Destiny
questions.explanation: Manifest Destiny helped justify expansion into western lands and war with Mexico.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map, 1846–1848. The map shows U.S. military campaigns in Mexico and western lands acquired after the war. Which political conflict intensified as a result of the territorial acquisition shown?

choices.A: Whether the United States should enter NATO
choices.B: Whether railroads should be nationalized
choices.C: Whether slavery would be permitted in lands acquired from Mexico
choices.D: Whether Prohibition should be repealed

questions.correctAnswerText: Whether slavery would be permitted in lands acquired from Mexico
questions.explanation: The Mexican Cession intensified conflict over slavery’s expansion.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map, 1846–1848. The map shows U.S. troops moving through northern Mexico, naval operations, U.S. forces entering California and New Mexico, and territory acquired after the war. Which argument is best supported?

choices.A: Territorial expansion strengthened national power while deepening conflicts over the meaning and limits of expansion.
choices.B: The Mexican-American War had no effect on domestic politics.
choices.C: Expansion after 1848 reduced sectional tensions over slavery.
choices.D: The war prevented the United States from reaching the Pacific.

questions.correctAnswerText: Territorial expansion strengthened national power while deepening conflicts over the meaning and limits of expansion.
questions.explanation: The war expanded U.S. territory but intensified sectional and ideological debates.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated Freedmen’s Bureau labor circular, 1866, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. “Contracts must be read aloud before signing. Wages, rations, and family residence must be stated, and planters may not use old authority to compel labor without agreement.” The circular’s historical situation is best explained by which development?

choices.A: Federal efforts to regulate the transition from slavery to free labor
choices.B: Southern state efforts to restore the international slave trade
choices.C: Northern attempts to end all cotton production
choices.D: British efforts to regulate colonial taxation

questions.correctAnswerText: Federal efforts to regulate the transition from slavery to free labor
questions.explanation: The circular’s contracts, wages, and limits on coercion reflect Freedmen’s Bureau labor policy.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated Freedmen’s Bureau labor circular, 1866, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The circular requires contracts to state wages, rations, and family residence and warns planters not to compel labor without agreement. Which claim is best supported?

choices.A: Bureau officials attempted to replace slavery with a contractual wage-labor system.
choices.B: The Bureau required freedpeople to work without pay.
choices.C: Planters lost all influence over labor after 1865.
choices.D: Reconstruction ended all disputes over rural work.

questions.correctAnswerText: Bureau officials attempted to replace slavery with a contractual wage-labor system.
questions.explanation: The circular requires written terms for wages, rations, and residence, showing contract labor ideals.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated Freedmen’s Bureau labor circular, 1866, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The circular emphasizes contracts, wages, rations, family residence, and limits on planter coercion. Which broader context best explains these concerns?

choices.A: The passage of the National Origins Act
choices.B: The end of slavery created conflict over labor rights, family autonomy, and federal authority in the South.
choices.C: The annexation of Hawaii created new plantation economies.
choices.D: The Great Awakening created new church hierarchies.

questions.correctAnswerText: The end of slavery created conflict over labor rights, family autonomy, and federal authority in the South.
questions.explanation: The circular addresses disputes over how free labor and family life would operate after emancipation.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated Freedmen’s Bureau labor circular, 1866, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The circular says contracts must be read aloud and must identify wages, rations, and family residence. Which argument about Reconstruction is best supported?

choices.A: Reconstruction involved not only political rights but also struggles over the practical meaning of free labor.
choices.B: Reconstruction ignored economic relations between freedpeople and planters.
choices.C: Reconstruction immediately created equal bargaining power in the rural South.
choices.D: Reconstruction had no relationship to family autonomy.

questions.correctAnswerText: Reconstruction involved not only political rights but also struggles over the practical meaning of free labor.
questions.explanation: The circular shows Reconstruction involved labor contracts, wages, family autonomy, and coercion.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map and production chart, c. 1860–1890. The map highlights Nevada’s Comstock Lode, Colorado mining districts, and rail links to San Francisco, Denver, and eastern markets. A chart shows rising silver output followed by unstable prices. Which conclusion is best supported?

choices.A: Western mining had no connection to national markets.
choices.B: Mining discoveries, railroads, and metal prices connected the West to national and global capitalism.
choices.C: Western mining ended all settlement in mountain regions.
choices.D: Silver production remained stable and politically unimportant.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mining discoveries, railroads, and metal prices connected the West to national and global capitalism.
questions.explanation: The map and chart link western mining to railroads, markets, and unstable commodity prices.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map and production chart, c. 1860–1890. Nevada’s Comstock Lode, Colorado mining districts, rail links, and changing silver output are shown. Which environmental or regional consequence most directly accompanied the development represented?

choices.A: Boomtown growth, landscape disruption, and intensified conflicts over western land and resources
choices.B: Complete preservation of western ecosystems from industrial extraction
choices.C: Decline of all railroad construction in mining regions
choices.D: Elimination of wage labor in the West

questions.correctAnswerText: Boomtown growth, landscape disruption, and intensified conflicts over western land and resources
questions.explanation: Mining brought rapid settlement, environmental change, and conflict over resources.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map and production chart, c. 1860–1890. The map shows western mining districts and rail links, while the chart shows rising silver output and unstable prices. Which political issue was most closely connected to silver production?

choices.A: Whether colonies should resist the Stamp Act
choices.B: Whether the federal government should create Medicare
choices.C: Whether the United States should enter World War I
choices.D: Debate over silver coinage and monetary policy

questions.correctAnswerText: Debate over silver coinage and monetary policy
questions.explanation: Silver production and prices contributed to late nineteenth-century monetary debates.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described map and production chart, c. 1860–1890. Nevada and Colorado mining districts, rail links to cities, and changing silver output are shown. Which broader pattern is most closely connected to this development?

choices.A: Westward migration driven by mineral discoveries and speculative investment
choices.B: Decline of all settlement west of the Mississippi
choices.C: Migration of Puritans to New England
choices.D: End of commercial agriculture in the Great Plains

questions.correctAnswerText: Westward migration driven by mineral discoveries and speculative investment
questions.explanation: Mining districts attracted migrants and investors to western regions.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated craft union notice, 1880s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. “Skilled workers must protect the dignity of their trades. We seek fair wages, shorter hours, and bargaining by organized craftsmen, not schemes that promise to remake all society at once.” Which organization’s approach is most directly reflected?

choices.A: The American Federation of Labor’s emphasis on skilled workers and practical workplace gains
choices.B: The Populist Party’s free-silver campaign
choices.C: The Social Gospel movement’s urban ministry
choices.D: The National Woman’s Party’s suffrage campaign

questions.correctAnswerText: The American Federation of Labor’s emphasis on skilled workers and practical workplace gains
questions.explanation: The notice’s focus on skilled craftsmen, wages, hours, and bargaining reflects AFL-style craft unionism.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated craft union notice, 1880s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The notice emphasizes skilled workers, trade dignity, wages, shorter hours, and bargaining by organized craftsmen. Which claim is best supported?

choices.A: Craft unions generally rejected workplace bargaining.
choices.B: Craft unionism often focused on wages, hours, and control of skilled labor.
choices.C: Craft unions primarily organized enslaved plantation workers.
choices.D: Craft unions avoided economic demands.

questions.correctAnswerText: Craft unionism often focused on wages, hours, and control of skilled labor.
questions.explanation: The source emphasizes practical workplace goals and organized skilled workers.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated craft union notice, 1880s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The notice supports organized skilled workers seeking wages, shorter hours, and bargaining, while rejecting broad schemes to remake society. Which argument about Gilded Age labor is best supported?

choices.A: Labor organizing included competing strategies, from broad reform visions to narrower craft bargaining.
choices.B: All labor organizations shared identical membership rules and goals.
choices.C: Skilled workers avoided union organization.
choices.D: Gilded Age labor activism focused only on farmers.

questions.correctAnswerText: Labor organizing included competing strategies, from broad reform visions to narrower craft bargaining.
questions.explanation: The notice contrasts craft bargaining with broader social transformation.

questions.prompt:
Stimulus: Plain-text table of Progressive political reforms.
Reform | Intended effect
Initiative | Allow voters to propose laws
Referendum | Allow voters to approve or reject laws
Recall | Allow voters to remove some officials before the end of a term
Direct primary | Reduce party boss control over nominations
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Progressive reforms reduced voter influence in politics.
choices.B: Some Progressives sought to make government more directly responsive to voters.
choices.C: Progressives wanted political parties to control all nominations without voter input.
choices.D: Progressive reforms abolished state governments.

questions.correctAnswerText: Some Progressives sought to make government more directly responsive to voters.
questions.explanation: Initiative, referendum, recall, and direct primaries increased voter influence.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of Progressive political reforms: initiative, referendum, recall, and direct primary were intended to give voters more influence over lawmaking, officeholding, and nominations. Which broader concern helped motivate these reforms?

choices.A: Fear that party machines, corporations, and political bosses had too much influence over government
choices.B: Desire to return all power to British colonial governors
choices.C: Support for ending popular elections
choices.D: Demand for immediate withdrawal from all overseas trade

questions.correctAnswerText: Fear that party machines, corporations, and political bosses had too much influence over government
questions.explanation: Progressive democratic reforms targeted corruption and concentrated political power.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of Progressive political reforms: initiative, referendum, recall, and direct primary were intended to make government more responsive to voters. Which movement was most closely connected to a similar expansion of democratic participation in the same era?

choices.A: The campaign to restore the Articles of Confederation
choices.B: The campaign for national Prohibition only
choices.C: The campaign against the Northwest Ordinance
choices.D: The woman suffrage movement

questions.correctAnswerText: The woman suffrage movement
questions.explanation: Woman suffrage was part of the broader Progressive-era expansion of democratic participation.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of Progressive political reforms: initiative, referendum, recall, and direct primary increased voter influence over laws, officials, and nominations. Which argument about Progressivism is best supported?

choices.A: Progressivism was only a business movement to reduce regulation.
choices.B: Progressives rejected democratic language in politics.
choices.C: Progressive reform often combined distrust of concentrated power with faith in informed voters and expert administration.
choices.D: Progressives avoided state and local government reform.

questions.correctAnswerText: Progressive reform often combined distrust of concentrated power with faith in informed voters and expert administration.
questions.explanation: The table reflects efforts to curb bosses and make politics more responsive.

questions.prompt:
Stimulus: Plain-text table of influenza and home-front conditions, 1918–1919.
Condition | Pattern
Troop movement | Large numbers moved through camps and ports
Urban public gatherings | Often restricted during outbreaks
War bond drives | Continued but sometimes modified
Public-health orders | Mask rules, school closures, and quarantines in some cities
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: The influenza pandemic intersected with wartime mobilization and local public-health measures.
choices.B: The influenza pandemic had no relationship to military movement.
choices.C: Public-health officials avoided restrictions during the pandemic.
choices.D: War bond campaigns ended permanently in 1918.

questions.correctAnswerText: The influenza pandemic intersected with wartime mobilization and local public-health measures.
questions.explanation: The table connects troop movement, public-health orders, public gatherings, and war bond campaigns.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of influenza and home-front conditions, 1918–1919. Troop movements through camps and ports were large; some cities restricted public gatherings, issued mask rules, closed schools, or used quarantines; war bond drives continued but were sometimes modified. Which broader context best explains the spread and response shown?

choices.A: The United States had ended all overseas military involvement by 1918.
choices.B: The Civil War draft riots shaped local public-health policy.
choices.C: World War I mobilization moved people rapidly while cities struggled to balance public health and wartime demands.
choices.D: New Deal relief agencies directed pandemic policy.

questions.correctAnswerText: World War I mobilization moved people rapidly while cities struggled to balance public health and wartime demands.
questions.explanation: Wartime troop movement and civic mobilization complicated pandemic response.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of influenza and home-front conditions, 1918–1919. Troop movement, restricted gatherings, modified bond drives, mask rules, school closures, and quarantines are shown. Which government role is most directly reflected?

choices.A: Federal courts using school busing to desegregate cities
choices.B: Local and state governments exercising public-health authority during emergency conditions
choices.C: State governments nullifying federal tariffs
choices.D: Congress admitting new states from the Northwest Territory

questions.correctAnswerText: Local and state governments exercising public-health authority during emergency conditions
questions.explanation: Mask rules, school closures, and quarantines show local and state public-health powers.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of influenza and home-front conditions, 1918–1919. Cities used school closures, quarantines, mask rules, and restrictions on gatherings while wartime mobilization continued. Which broader historical pattern is most closely connected?

choices.A: Public crises often expand debate over the balance between individual behavior, community welfare, and government authority.
choices.B: Public-health emergencies always eliminate political debate.
choices.C: Military mobilization prevents local governments from acting.
choices.D: Epidemics had no social effects in twentieth-century cities.

questions.correctAnswerText: Public crises often expand debate over the balance between individual behavior, community welfare, and government authority.
questions.explanation: Pandemic restrictions reveal tensions among rights, public welfare, and state authority.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described classroom poster, late 1950s. The poster shows a satellite orbiting Earth above students working with microscopes, slide rules, and mathematics textbooks. A caption reads, “Science strengthens freedom.” Which historical situation is most directly reflected?

choices.A: The decline of federal support for education during the Cold War
choices.B: Opposition to all scientific research after World War II
choices.C: Cold War concern that science and education were essential to national security
choices.D: The First Great Awakening’s emphasis on religious revival

questions.correctAnswerText: Cold War concern that science and education were essential to national security
questions.explanation: The poster links science education to freedom and Cold War competition.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described classroom poster, late 1950s. A satellite orbits Earth above students using microscopes, slide rules, and mathematics textbooks; the caption reads, “Science strengthens freedom.” Which event most directly intensified the concern reflected in the poster?

choices.A: The Soviet launch of Sputnik
choices.B: The passage of the Missouri Compromise
choices.C: The publication of the Monroe Doctrine
choices.D: The Nullification Crisis

questions.correctAnswerText: The Soviet launch of Sputnik
questions.explanation: Sputnik intensified U.S. fears about science, technology, education, and Cold War competition.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described classroom poster, late 1950s. A satellite appears above students using scientific and mathematical tools, and the caption says, “Science strengthens freedom.” Which federal response belongs to this context?

choices.A: The Chinese Exclusion Act
choices.B: The Freedmen’s Bureau
choices.C: The Alien and Sedition Acts
choices.D: Increased federal support for science and mathematics education

questions.correctAnswerText: Increased federal support for science and mathematics education
questions.explanation: The federal government increased support for science and math education after Sputnik.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Described classroom poster, late 1950s. A satellite orbits above students using microscopes, slide rules, and mathematics textbooks; the caption reads, “Science strengthens freedom.” Which argument about the Cold War is best supported?

choices.A: Cold War competition shaped domestic education and cultural definitions of citizenship.
choices.B: Cold War policy had no effect on schools.
choices.C: Americans viewed science education as unrelated to national identity.
choices.D: The space race ended all federal involvement in education.

questions.correctAnswerText: Cold War competition shaped domestic education and cultural definitions of citizenship.
questions.explanation: The poster connects classroom science to national identity and Cold War freedom.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated LGBTQ+ rights leaflet, early 1970s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. “We are tired of hiding from police raids, employers, and neighbors. Public life belongs to us too: streets, bars, jobs, newspapers, and families.” Which historical situation is most directly reflected?

choices.A: LGBTQ+ activism emerging from resistance to policing and demands for public recognition
choices.B: The creation of the National Origins Act
choices.C: The campaign for the Missouri Compromise
choices.D: The expansion of Spanish missions in New Mexico

questions.correctAnswerText: LGBTQ+ activism emerging from resistance to policing and demands for public recognition
questions.explanation: The leaflet objects to police raids, discrimination, and hiding while claiming public space.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated LGBTQ+ rights leaflet, early 1970s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The leaflet states that LGBTQ+ people are tired of hiding from police raids, employers, and neighbors and claims public life in streets, bars, jobs, newspapers, and families. Which claim is best supported?

choices.A: LGBTQ+ activists connected personal identity to public space, employment, media, and family recognition.
choices.B: LGBTQ+ activism avoided public protest.
choices.C: LGBTQ+ activists focused only on foreign policy.
choices.D: LGBTQ+ rights were fully protected by federal law in 1970.

questions.correctAnswerText: LGBTQ+ activists connected personal identity to public space, employment, media, and family recognition.
questions.explanation: The leaflet lists streets, bars, jobs, newspapers, and families as areas of public recognition.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Original simulated LGBTQ+ rights leaflet, early 1970s, based on historical context, not an actual quotation. The leaflet objects to police raids, discrimination, and forced hiding while demanding public recognition. Which broader pattern does the leaflet reflect?

choices.A: The decline of rights-based activism after 1945
choices.B: The expansion of social movements that challenged legal exclusion and cultural stigma
choices.C: The end of all debates over sexuality and citizenship
choices.D: The disappearance of urban protest movements

questions.correctAnswerText: The expansion of social movements that challenged legal exclusion and cultural stigma
questions.explanation: The leaflet reflects postwar rights movements challenging discrimination and stigma.

questions.prompt:
Stimulus: Plain-text table of economic change in North America.
Pattern | Late 20th-century development
Trade agreements | Reduced some barriers to cross-border commerce
Manufacturing employment | Declined in some older industrial communities
Service and logistics work | Expanded in many metropolitan regions
Corporate supply chains | Became more international
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Late twentieth-century economic integration had uneven effects across regions and sectors.
choices.B: Trade agreements restored manufacturing employment evenly in all regions.
choices.C: Corporate supply chains became less international after 1980.
choices.D: Service and logistics work disappeared as trade expanded.

questions.correctAnswerText: Late twentieth-century economic integration had uneven effects across regions and sectors.
questions.explanation: The table shows cross-border trade, international supply chains, service growth, and manufacturing decline in some communities.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of economic change in North America: trade agreements reduced some cross-border barriers, manufacturing declined in some older industrial communities, service and logistics work expanded in many metropolitan regions, and corporate supply chains became more international. Which policy development belongs most directly to this context?

choices.A: The Northwest Ordinance
choices.B: NAFTA and debates over free trade
choices.C: The Stamp Act
choices.D: The Missouri Compromise

questions.correctAnswerText: NAFTA and debates over free trade
questions.explanation: NAFTA belongs directly to late twentieth-century debates over North American economic integration.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of economic change in North America: trade agreements reduced some barriers, manufacturing declined in some older industrial communities, service and logistics work expanded, and corporate supply chains became more international. Which social consequence was most directly connected to these changes?

choices.A: Regional disagreement over whether globalization created opportunity or economic insecurity
choices.B: Complete disappearance of debates over trade policy
choices.C: Uniform wage gains for all manufacturing workers
choices.D: The return of most workers to subsistence farming

questions.correctAnswerText: Regional disagreement over whether globalization created opportunity or economic insecurity
questions.explanation: The table’s mixed effects help explain regional and political disagreement over globalization.

questions.prompt: Stimulus: Plain-text table of economic change in North America: trade agreements reduced some barriers, manufacturing declined in some older industrial communities, service and logistics work expanded, and corporate supply chains became more international. Which argument about recent U.S. history is best supported?

choices.A: Globalization had no connection to domestic political debate.
choices.B: Free-trade policy ended regional economic inequality.
choices.C: Economic globalization reshaped work and politics by creating both benefits and dislocations.
choices.D: Manufacturing communities were unaffected by international competition.

questions.correctAnswerText: Economic globalization reshaped work and politics by creating both benefits and dislocations.
questions.explanation: The table supports an argument that globalization produced opportunities and disruptions.
